fact: March 28 sits exactly one week after the spring equinox, which means day and night are still battling for control but daylight is winning by about an hour!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for earthworms to go absolutely bonkers - they're wiggling up from deep soil as it warms, making birds throw the ultimate worm party.
Did you know that in Antarctica, March 28 marks the beginning of serious darkness? The sun is setting earlier each day as winter approaches down south.
Maple trees are basically sugar factories on this date - sap is flowing like crazy in cold regions, perfect for making maple syrup!
March 28 is day 87 of the year in regular years and day 88 in leap years, which means you've survived almost exactly one-quarter of the entire year.
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are fully awake and hungry by now - they've been up for weeks stuffing their faces after their long winter naps.
The moon phase around March 28 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, the night sky becomes a spectacular star-watching playground.
In many places, March 28 brings the first real warm day where you can wear shorts - soil temperature hits that magic 50°F mark that makes grass grow like it's been drinking energy drinks.
Dandelions are secretly preparing their yellow invasion around this time - their roots are pumping up with energy to create those bright flowers that will soon cover every lawn.
March 28 falls during Aries season in astrology, when the ram constellation is strutting across the night sky like it owns the place.
In the Southern Hemisphere, leaves are starting their color-changing magic show as autumn kicks in - it's like nature's own fireworks display in slow motion.
